title: Fitting a thermostatic bath filler tap
subtitle: Replace a standard bath tap with a deck-mounted thermostatic mixer
excerpt: How to remove an existing bath tap or tap pair and fit a deck-mounted thermostatic mixer tap, covering the removal of old fittings, pipe preparation, connection to the hot and cold supplies, and setting the maximum temperature limit on the thermostatic cartridge.
sourceNotes: WRAS guidance on thermostatic mixing valves. CIPHE Plumbers Handbook. NHS HTM 04-01 (guidance on Legionella and thermostatic control in domestic settings). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature-sensitive valve mechanism inside a thermostatic mixer tap, containing a wax element or bimetallic spring that responds to outlet water temperature and automatically adjusts the balance of hot and cold flow to maintain a pre-set temperature. The cartridge also incorporates a failsafe that shuts off flow if the cold supply fails, preventing scalding. Most cartridges have an adjustable maximum temperature limiter, typically factory-set at 38°C to 43°C for bath filling.

content.text: A thermostatic bath filler maintains a constant outlet temperature regardless of fluctuations in the hot or cold supply pressure. The

also shuts off if the cold supply fails, so the tap will not deliver scalding water to an unattended bath. This makes them particularly useful in households with children or elderly users. Fitting is a straightforward tap swap job; no new pipework is required if the existing hot and cold supplies terminate near the bath deck.

Deck-mounted thermostatic bath mixer tap (confirm the deck hole centres match the existing bath, standard UK spacing is 180 mm centres for a two-hole tap). Flexible tap connectors with isolation valves (typically ½-inch threaded to 15 mm compression or push-fit; supplied with most tap kits). PTFE tape. Silicone sealant for sealing the tap base to the bath deck.
Step 1, isolate and remove old taps

Close the isolation valves on the hot and cold supply pipes under the bath, or close the relevant service valves at the boiler and cold tank. Open the old taps to drain residual pressure. Use the water-pump pliers to grip the back nut on the underside of each old tap and unscrew. Remove the tap and the old flexible connectors. Clean the bath deck around the tap holes.
Step 2, fit the new tap body

Apply a thin bead of silicone sealant around the underside of the tap base plate to seal against water ingress into the deck. Feed the tap tails down through the bath deck holes. Below the bath, fit the backing plate and back nut and tighten by hand, then with the adjustable spanner until the tap base is held firmly without rocking.
Step 3, connect the hot and cold supplies

Connect the flexible tap connectors to the tap tails (hand-tight, then one-quarter turn with the spanner). Connect the other end of each flexible connector to the hot and cold supply pipes using compression or push-fit connections as appropriate. Hot supply connects to the hot tail (marked H or red); cold supply connects to the cold tail (marked C or blue). Reversing these connections will prevent the thermostatic cartridge from regulating temperature correctly. Restore supply, purge air through the tap, and check for leaks.
Step 4, set the maximum temperature limit

Most thermostatic bath taps have an adjustable temperature limiter under the tap handle or under a removable cap. Fill the bath and check the outlet temperature with a thermometer: for a family bathroom the standard is 43°C to 46°C maximum. Adjust the limiter stop screw to restrict the maximum temperature. For households with vulnerable users, set the maximum at 43°C.

attrs.symptom: Outlet temperature fluctuates widely and does not stabilise
attrs.cause: The hot supply pressure is significantly higher or lower than the cold supply pressure, which can overwhelm the thermostatic cartridge's balancing range.
attrs.fix: Thermostatic cartridges are designed for balanced pressure systems. If the hot supply is unvented (mains-pressure) and the cold is from a cold water tank (gravity-fed), fit a pressure-balancing valve on the cold inlet to equalise supply pressures.

attrs.symptom: Tap delivers cold water only, even at maximum temperature setting
attrs.cause: Hot and cold connections have been reversed.
attrs.fix: Isolate, drain, and swap the flexible tap connector connections at the supply end.
